symptoms.symptom_name: Sudden-onset palpitations (racing, pounding heartbeat) triggered by a stressor

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Stress-triggered sudden palpitations are typical of panic, but are non-specific.

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: paroxysmal supraventricular tachycardia or rapid atrial fibrillation
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Palpitations are the cardinal symptom of a tachyarrhythmia; a stress trigger does not exclude one.
Palpitations occur in thyrotoxicosis, but the free hormones are normal here (subclinical).

symptoms.symptom_name: Fine resting tremor of both hands with a sense of impending doom

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A fine tremor with a sense of impending doom during an acute stress surge is a classic panic constellation.
Fine tremor is also seen in thyrotoxicosis; overlap is precisely what makes the case a red herring.

symptoms.symptom_name: Acute severe anxiety following an interpersonal stressor

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: An identifiable acute psychosocial precipitant strongly supports a panic attack.

symptoms.symptom_name: Mild shortness of breath, able to speak in full sentences

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Subjective breathlessness with preserved speech fits hyperventilation in panic.
Dyspnoea is non-specific; it neither confirms nor excludes a cardiac cause.
A rate of ~105 is consistent with anxiety-driven sinus tachycardia, but a wearable does not report rhythm and is patient-obtained and unverified.

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: paroxysmal supraventricular tachycardia or rapid atrial fibrillation
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A rate of ~105 is below the usual SVT range (>150), making a re-entrant tachycardia less likely — but this cannot be relied upon without an ECG.
Somatic hypervigilance and repeated pulse-checking perpetuate the panic cycle.

symptoms.symptom_name: Rapid pressured speech and visible restlessness

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Psychomotor agitation and pressured speech are video-observable features of an acute anxiety state.

symptoms.symptom_name: Known suppressed TSH with normal free hormones and positive TSH-receptor antibodies

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Low TSH with normal free T4/T3 and positive TRAb defines subclinical Graves' disease.
The known thyroid status is the red herring — it explains a lowered threshold for adrenergic symptoms but is not the acute driver.

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: paroxysmal supraventricular tachycardia or rapid atrial fibrillation
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Subclinical hyperthyroidism modestly increases atrial fibrillation risk, which is why an ECG is warranted.

symptoms.symptom_name: No central/crushing or radiating chest pain (pertinent negative)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of ischaemic-quality chest pain lowers ACS probability but does not exclude it without an ECG.

symptoms.symptom_name: No fever, delirium or profuse diaphoresis (pertinent negative)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Afebrile status with clear mentation and no profound diaphoresis makes thyroid storm very unlikely.

symptoms.symptom_name: Regular sinus tachycardia at ~105 bpm on auscultation (bedside finding)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A regular sinus tachycardia (not >150, not irregularly irregular) is the sealed bedside finding that excludes SVT/rapid AF — unavailable over telehealth, which is why an ECG must be arranged.

symptoms.symptom_name: 12-lead ECG (expected: sinus tachycardia, no ischaemia, no arrhythmia)

investigation_details.expected_result: regular sinus tachycardia without ischaemic ST/T changes and without a tachyarrhythmia
investigation_details.if_not_ordered_consequence: Without an ECG, a tachyarrhythmia and acute coronary syndrome cannot be excluded and a remote 'benign panic' verdict is unsafe.

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: An ECG (with troponin) is required to exclude ACS in a patient reporting a 'heart attack' sensation.

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: paroxysmal supraventricular tachycardia or rapid atrial fibrillation
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Only an ECG can confirm regular sinus rhythm and exclude a tachyarrhythmia.
A normal temperature is a key sealed measurement arguing against thyroid storm.

symptom_clusters.clinical_significance: Stress-triggered palpitations, tremor, breathlessness, doom, pressured speech and hypervigilance together strongly favour an acute panic attack once dangerous mimics are screened.

investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: First-line test to characterise the rhythm and exclude a tachyarrhythmia and acute ischaemia — the exclusion telehealth cannot perform.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Cardiac troponin (if ongoing chest symptoms or abnormal ECG)
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Adjunct to the ECG to exclude acute coronary syndrome where clinical or ECG concern persists.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Thyroid function tests (TSH, free T4, free T3)
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: GP follow-up to re-confirm subclinical status and monitor for progression to overt thyrotoxicosis; not an acute-setting test.
Not indicated for this acute presentation; imaging the thyroid does nothing to address the acute symptoms or the cardiac exclusion.
